questions.question:
Nobody knows how the Indian cricket team is going to cope with the difficult and seamer-friendly wickets in Australia.

Choose the option which is closest in meaning to the underlined phrase in the above sentence.

A. Put up with
B. Put in
C. Put down
D. Put up against
questions.to_find: option closest in meaning to 'cope with'

understand.plain: The phrase 'cope with' means to deal successfully with a difficult situation. We need to find a phrasal verb among the options that carries the same sense of managing or enduring something challenging. 'Put up with' is the phrasal verb that means to tolerate or deal with something difficult, making it the closest match.

keywords.explain: to manage, deal with, or endure something difficult
keywords.example: Athletes must cope with pressure during major tournaments.

keywords.explain: a verb combined with a preposition or adverb that together carry a meaning different from the individual words
keywords.example: 'Put up with' means to tolerate, not literally to put something up against something.

keywords.explain: cricket pitches that help fast bowlers who rely on the seam of the ball; extra context showing the difficulty to be endured
keywords.example: Seamer-friendly conditions in England or Australia challenge batsmen.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this vocabulary question.

given.aim: Identify the phrasal verb among the four options that is synonymous with 'cope with'

terms.meaning: to deal with or manage something difficult
terms.example: She had to cope with the unexpected change in plans.
terms.connects: the target phrase whose synonym we seek

terms.meaning: to tolerate or deal with something unpleasant or challenging
terms.example: He had to put up with long hours at work.
terms.connects: option A — the synonym for 'cope with'

terms.meaning: to place in opposition or competition with something
terms.example: The team was put up against a stronger opponent.
terms.connects: option D — means opposition, not endurance

terms.meaning: to suppress, write down, or disparage something
terms.example: The rebellion was put down swiftly.
terms.connects: option C — entirely different meaning from 'cope with'

given.plan: Eliminate options by checking whether the phrasal verb matches 'deal with difficulty': 'put in' means to insert; 'put down' means to suppress; 'put up against' means to oppose; only 'put up with' means to endure or manage.

steps.title: Understand the target phrase
steps.apply: 'Cope with' = to manage or endure a difficult situation. The sentence says the team must manage difficult, seamer-friendly conditions in Australia.
steps.note: The context confirms a sense of struggle and endurance, not placement or opposition.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A. Put up with = tolerate/deal with (MATCH). B. Put in = insert or contribute (NO MATCH). C. Put down = suppress/write (NO MATCH). D. Put up against = face as an opponent (NO MATCH).
steps.note: Only 'put up with' carries the meaning of dealing with or enduring a difficult situation.

solution.result: Option A — 'Put up with' is closest in meaning to 'cope with'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula required; this is a vocabulary/phrasal-verb equivalence question.

questions.question:
I do not think you know the case well enough to have opinions. Having said that, I agree with your other point.

What does the phrase 'having said that' mean in the given text?

A. as opposed to what I have said
B. despite what I have said
C. in addition to what I have said
D. contrary to what I have said
questions.answer: B. despite what I have said
questions.to_find: meaning of the phrase 'having said that' in context

understand.plain: 'Having said that' is a discourse marker used to introduce a statement that partially contrasts with what was just said. The speaker first states a criticism (you don't know the case well enough) and then, using 'having said that', concedes agreement on another point. This 'concede despite earlier criticism' function is best captured by 'despite what I have said'.

keywords.explain: a word or phrase that organises speech or text, showing a logical or rhetorical relationship between ideas
keywords.example: 'However', 'moreover', and 'having said that' are all discourse markers.

keywords.explain: acknowledging a prior statement while introducing something that partially contradicts or qualifies it
keywords.example: 'It was raining. Having said that, we still enjoyed the picnic.' — the enjoyment holds despite the rain.

keywords.explain: in spite of; without being prevented by
keywords.example: 'Despite the delay, the project was a success.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a reading-comprehension vocabulary question.

given.aim: Select the option that accurately captures the concessive function of 'having said that'

terms.meaning: a phrase used to introduce a concession — something true despite what was just expressed
terms.example: The plan is risky. Having said that, the potential reward is enormous.
terms.connects: target phrase whose meaning must be identified

terms.meaning: in direct opposition or contrast — a much stronger adversative relation
terms.example: 'I prefer tea as opposed to coffee.'
terms.connects: options A and D — too strong; 'having said that' concedes rather than flatly opposes

terms.meaning: supplementing or adding to what was already said
terms.example: 'She is talented; in addition to that, she works hard.'
terms.connects: option C — additive, not concessive; wrong relationship

terms.meaning: in spite of the criticism or limitation just expressed
terms.example: 'You may not be qualified enough; despite that, I trust your instinct.'
terms.connects: option B — correctly captures the concessive function

given.plan: Identify the logical relationship: the speaker criticises first, then partially agrees. 'Having said that' bridges them with a concession — 'despite' what was just stated.

steps.title: Identify the two-part structure
steps.apply: Part 1 (criticism): 'I do not think you know the case well enough to have opinions.' Part 2 (concession): 'I agree with your other point.' The bridge phrase 'having said that' links these two contrasting ideas.
steps.note: The key relationship is: strong criticism followed by a partial acknowledgement.

steps.title: Match the relationship to the options
steps.apply: A — 'as opposed to' means outright contrast, not a concession. C — 'in addition to' implies supplementation, not contrast. D — 'contrary to' is a direct contradiction, not a concession. B — 'despite what I have said' correctly signals: I agree with your point even though my prior statement was critical.
steps.note: 'Having said that' is a standard concessive marker equivalent to 'nevertheless' or 'that being said'.

solution.result: Option B — 'despite what I have said' is the correct meaning of 'having said that' in this context.

questions.formula_note: No formula required; this question tests knowledge of discourse markers and concessive conjunctions.

questions.question:
Choose the most suitable one word substitute for the following expression:

Connotation of a road or way

A. Pertinacious
B. Viaticum
C. Clandestine
D. Ravenous
questions.to_find: one-word substitute for 'connotation of a road or way'

understand.plain: The expression asks for a single word whose meaning relates to a road or a journey along a way. 'Viaticum' comes from the Latin 'via' meaning 'road' or 'way', and historically refers to provisions or money for a journey (and also the Eucharist given to the dying as provisions for their final journey). Among the options it is the only word etymologically and semantically connected to the concept of a road or way.

keywords.explain: from Latin 'via' (road/way); refers to provisions for a journey or, in a religious context, the last Eucharist before death (the final journey); the word is rooted in the concept of a road or way
keywords.example: The traveller was given viaticum — food and money — before setting out on the long road.

keywords.explain: stubbornly persistent; holding firmly to a purpose or opinion
keywords.example: Her pertinacious effort finally paid off after years of work.

keywords.explain: kept secret or done in a secretive manner
keywords.example: They held a clandestine meeting in an abandoned warehouse.
After the long hike, the team was ravenous for food.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this one-word substitution question.

given.aim: Identify the word whose core meaning is related to 'a road or way'

terms.term: connotation of a road or way
terms.meaning: the concept or association centred on roads, paths, or journeys
terms.example: A word derived from Latin 'via' carries the connotation of a road.
terms.connects: the defining phrase we need to match with a single word

terms.meaning: supplies or allowance for a journey; etymologically from Latin via (road)
terms.example: Medieval pilgrims carried their viaticum on long religious journeys.
terms.connects: option B — correct answer; 'via' = road/way is its root

terms.meaning: stubbornly resolute; from Latin 'pertinax' meaning persistent
terms.example: His pertinacious nature helped him overcome every obstacle.
terms.connects: option A — no connection to roads

terms.meaning: secretive; from Latin 'clam' (secretly)
terms.example: Their clandestine operation was finally uncovered.
terms.connects: option C — no connection to roads

given.plan: Check each word's etymology and core meaning against the phrase 'road or way'. The Latin root 'via' (road) directly appears in 'viaticum', making it the sole match.

steps.title: Identify the key semantic requirement
steps.apply: We need a word that means or connotes 'road' or 'way'. The Latin root 'via' means road, and words built on it carry that connotation.
steps.note: Looking at etymology is the fastest route when multiple unfamiliar English words appear as options.

steps.title: Evaluate each option against the requirement
steps.apply: A. Pertinacious = stubborn/persistent (root: pertinax). B. Viaticum = provisions for a journey (root: via = road). C. Clandestine = secret (root: clam = secretly). D. Ravenous = extremely hungry (root: raven/rapine). Only B relates to a road or way.
steps.note: Viaticum is also used in Catholic tradition for the last Eucharist — literally 'provisions for the final road'.

solution.result: Option B — 'Viaticum' is the one-word substitute for 'connotation of a road or way'.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula required; this is a vocabulary etymology question.

questions.question:
Select the appropriate option in place of underlined part of the sentence.

Increased productivity necessary reflects greater efforts made by the employees.

A. Increase in productivity necessary
B. Increase productivity is necessary
C. Increase in productivity necessarily
D. No improvement required
questions.to_find: grammatically and semantically correct replacement for the underlined phrase 'Increased productivity necessary'

understand.plain: The original sentence uses 'necessary' (an adjective) where an adverb is needed to modify the verb 'reflects'. The correct word is 'necessarily' (adverb). Additionally, 'increased productivity' can be replaced by the noun phrase 'increase in productivity' for better formal expression, though the key fix is changing the adjective 'necessary' to the adverb 'necessarily'.

keywords.explain: an adjective modifies a noun (necessary plan), while an adverb modifies a verb, adjective, or another adverb (necessarily reflects)
keywords.example: 'He worked quick' is wrong; 'He worked quickly' is correct — the adverb modifies the verb.

keywords.explain: the adverb form of 'necessary'; means 'as a direct or inevitable result'; used to modify verbs
keywords.example: 'Higher prices do not necessarily mean better quality.'

keywords.explain: the noun phrase correctly naming a rise in productive output; more idiomatic than 'increased productivity' in this context
keywords.example: 'An increase in productivity was observed after the training.'

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this grammar-correction question.

given.aim: Replace the underlined segment with the option that makes the sentence grammatically and logically correct

terms.meaning: original underlined phrase — adjective 'necessary' incorrectly modifies the verb 'reflects'
terms.example: The sentence as written is grammatically incorrect because 'necessary' is an adjective, not an adverb.
terms.connects: target phrase that must be corrected

terms.meaning: adverb meaning 'as an inevitable consequence'; correct word to modify the verb 'reflects'
terms.example: 'This increase necessarily reflects the hard work put in by the team.'
terms.connects: option C — contains the adverb form required by grammar

terms.meaning: standard noun-phrase form for a rise in output
terms.example: 'An increase in productivity is always welcome.'
terms.connects: used in options A, B, C — a better noun phrase than 'increased productivity' in this context

terms.meaning: option D — the sentence does not already sound correct; improvement is needed
terms.example: The original sentence has a clear grammatical error, ruling out option D.
terms.connects: distractor; the sentence is not grammatically correct as given

given.plan: The core error is adjective ('necessary') used where an adverb ('necessarily') is needed. Find the option that uses 'necessarily' and also has the correct noun phrase structure.

steps.title: Diagnose the grammatical error
steps.apply: In 'Increased productivity necessary reflects greater efforts', the word 'necessary' is an adjective modifying nothing correctly; the verb 'reflects' needs an adverb modifier. The correct form is 'necessarily'.
steps.note: Adverbs modify verbs; adjectives modify nouns. This is a standard part-of-speech error.

steps.title: Screen options for the adverb
steps.apply: A uses 'necessary' (adjective) — still wrong. B uses 'is necessary' — restructures the sentence incorrectly. C uses 'necessarily' (adverb) — correct! D claims no improvement needed — wrong, as there is an error. Therefore C is correct.
steps.note: Corrected sentence: 'Increase in productivity necessarily reflects greater efforts made by the employees.'

solution.result: Option C — 'Increase in productivity necessarily' is the correct replacement.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula required; this is a grammar (adjective vs adverb) correction question.

questions.question:
Rescue teams deployed _______ disaster hit areas combat _______ a lot of difficulties to save the people.

A. with, at
B. in, with
C. with, with
D. to, to
questions.to_find: correct pair of prepositions to fill the two blanks

understand.plain: The sentence has two blanks requiring prepositions: one after 'deployed' (to indicate location — in disaster-hit areas) and one after 'combat' (to indicate against what — combat with difficulties). Teams are 'deployed in' an area, and you 'combat with' difficulties. The correct pair is 'in, with'.

keywords.explain: rescue teams are stationed or sent into a location; 'in' is the correct preposition for placement inside an area
keywords.example: Soldiers were deployed in the border regions.

keywords.meaning: to fight against or struggle against; 'with' indicates what one is struggling against
keywords.explain: to fight or struggle with something means to face it as an adversary
keywords.example: The doctors had to combat with a new strain of the virus.

keywords.term: preposition of location vs preposition of struggle
keywords.explain: different actions require different prepositions: location uses 'in/at/on', struggle uses 'with/against'
keywords.example: 'Work in a city' (location) vs 'deal with a problem' (struggle).

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed for this preposition selection question.

given.aim: Choose the preposition pair that correctly fills blank 1 (after 'deployed') and blank 2 (after 'combat')

terms.meaning: the teams are located/sent inside those areas; requires a locative preposition
terms.example: Troops were deployed in the flood-affected zone.
terms.connects: blank 1 — 'in' is correct for location inside an area

terms.meaning: to struggle or fight against difficulties; 'with' signals the adversary of the struggle
terms.example: They combatted with hunger and disease during the crisis.
terms.connects: blank 2 — 'with' is the correct preposition with 'combat'

terms.meaning: accompaniment or struggle — 'deployed with' would mean accompanied by, not located inside
terms.example: 'The team arrived with equipment' vs 'was deployed in the area'.
terms.connects: option A and C use 'with' for blank 1 — incorrect; 'in' is needed for location

terms.meaning: point in space or time; less fitting than 'with' when describing what is being struggled against
terms.example: 'Aimed at a target' vs 'combatted with obstacles'.
terms.connects: option A uses 'at' for blank 2 — possible but 'with' is more idiomatic with 'combat'

given.plan: For blank 1: teams are 'deployed in' a region (location). For blank 2: 'combat with' is the idiomatic phrase for struggling against difficulties. Match to option B: in, with.

steps.title: Fill blank 1: preposition after 'deployed'
steps.apply: Rescue teams are stationed or sent into disaster areas. The correct preposition for being stationed inside a zone is 'in'. 'Deployed in disaster hit areas' is the standard usage. Options A and C use 'with' (means accompaniment, not location) — eliminated. Option D uses 'to' (implies movement toward, not presence in) — less accurate here.

steps.title: Fill blank 2: preposition after 'combat'
steps.apply: The teams face and struggle against 'a lot of difficulties'. The idiomatic expression is 'combat with' (struggle with). Option A uses 'at' — not standard with 'combat'. Option B uses 'with' — correct. This gives the pair: in, with.
steps.note: Corrected sentence: 'Rescue teams deployed in disaster hit areas combat with a lot of difficulties to save the people.'

solution.result: Option B — 'in, with' is the correct pair of prepositions.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula required; this question tests knowledge of collocational prepositions.

questions.question:
The line ran _________ the page, right through the centre, and divided the page into two.

A. across
B. of
C. between
D. about
questions.to_find: correct preposition to describe a line running through the width of the page

understand.plain: The sentence describes a line that traverses the full width of the page, passing right through its centre and splitting it into two halves. The preposition 'across' precisely captures this idea of lateral movement from one side to the other. No other option fits: 'of' is a genitive preposition, 'between' requires two distinct objects, and 'about' suggests approximation.

keywords.explain: from one side to the other; indicating traversal of a surface from edge to edge
keywords.example: 'A bridge runs across the river' — it connects both sides.

keywords.explain: in the space separating two distinct things; requires two specific referents
keywords.example: 'Between the two cities' — there must be two named cities.

keywords.explain: approximately, or on the subject of; does not indicate spatial traversal
keywords.example: 'A book about history' — topic, not direction.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='320' height='160' viewBox='0 0 320 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='280' height='120' rx='6' class='bg'/><line x1='20' y1='80' x2='300' y2='80' stroke='var(--no,#D85A30)' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><text x='155' y='52' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Page</text><text x='155' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Line runs ACROSS the page</text><text x='28' y='84' class='sub mut'>|</text><text x='290' y='84' text-anchor='end' class='sub mut'>|</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A rectangle representing a page with a dashed line running horizontally from left edge to right edge through the centre, illustrating 'across'.

given.aim: Select the preposition that correctly expresses a line traversing the full width of the page through its centre

terms.meaning: the line moved from one edge of the page to the other, covering the page's width
terms.example: A crack ran across the ceiling from one wall to the other.
terms.connects: the blank that needs a preposition indicating traversal

terms.meaning: from one side to the other; traversing the full width of a surface
terms.example: The equator runs across the globe.
terms.connects: option A — correct; captures side-to-side traversal

terms.meaning: in the intervening space of two distinct objects
terms.example: A path runs between the two houses.
terms.connects: option C — incorrect here; 'a line between the page' is grammatically odd and needs two referents

terms.meaning: approximately, or concerning a topic
terms.example: A story about courage.
terms.connects: option D — incorrect; does not describe a spatial path

given.plan: Use the visual image of a line drawn from the left edge to the right edge of a page. The preposition that best describes this traversal action is 'across'. Verify by substitution: 'The line ran across the page, right through the centre' reads naturally and is grammatically correct.

steps.title: Identify the spatial relationship being described
steps.apply: The line divides the page into two — this means it goes from one side to the other, right through the centre. This is the spatial concept of traversal: side to side.
steps.note: The phrase 'right through the centre' confirms a full traversal, not just a nearby position.

steps.title: Match traversal concept to the correct preposition
steps.apply: A. across — from one side to the other (CORRECT). B. of — genitive relation, not spatial (WRONG). C. between — requires two separate objects (WRONG). D. about — approximation or topic (WRONG). Substitution check: 'The line ran across the page, right through the centre, and divided the page into two.' — grammatically perfect.
steps.note: Classic usage: 'draw a line across the page' is standard in both everyday and academic writing.

solution.result: Option A — 'across' is the correct preposition.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formula required; this question tests knowledge of spatial prepositions.
